title: Chicken and Dumplings
subtitle: Southern American chicken stew with fluffy drop dumplings steamed in the broth
description: Chicken and dumplings is quintessential Southern American comfort food: a thick chicken broth with poached chicken, vegetables, and soft drop dumplings that cook by steaming on top of the simmering stew. The dumplings are made from a simple batter of flour, baking powder, butter, and milk — dropped by the spoonful onto the surface of the stew, then covered and cooked without disturbing for fifteen minutes. The result is something between a soup and a stew.
sourceNotes: Chicken and dumplings is deeply embedded in Southern American and Appalachian cooking traditions. The drop-dumpling style appears in Fannie Farmer's The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896) and has remained largely unchanged in American home cooking through the twentieth century.

recipeTools.notes: With a tight-fitting lid — the dumplings cook by steam, so the lid must seal during the dumpling stage.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dumpling made from a wet, spoonable batter dropped directly onto simmering liquid and cooked with a lid on. The steam cooks the top while the bottom absorbs flavour from the broth. Distinct from rolled or filled dumplings.

ingredients.notes: Or 1 kg boneless thighs — the whole chicken gives better broth
Finely chopped — optional, stirred into the batter

steps: If using a whole chicken: place it in the casserole with the stock, thyme, and bay leaves. Bring to a simmer and cook for 35–40 minutes until cooked through. Remove the chicken, shred the meat from the bones, and discard the bones and skin. Strain and reserve the broth.
If using boneless thighs: skip the first step — just proceed with the broth.
Melt the butter in the casserole over a medium heat. Cook the onion, celery, and carrot for 6–7 minutes until softened. Add the garlic for 1 minute. Scatter over the flour and stir for 1–2 minutes.

Pour in the reserved chicken broth (or the fresh hot stock if using boneless thighs) gradually, stirring to prevent lumps. Add the thyme and bay leaves (if not already used). Bring to a simmer and cook for 10 minutes until the broth has thickened slightly.
Return the shredded chicken to the pot. Stir in the cream and peas. Taste and adjust seasoning. Keep at a gentle simmer.

Make the dumpling batter: whisk together the flour, baking powder, and salt. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles rough breadcrumbs. Stir in the milk and parsley, if using, until just combined — the batter should be wet and shaggy.
Drop heaped tablespoons of the batter onto the surface of the simmering stew, spacing them slightly apart.
Cover the pot tightly and cook for 15 minutes without lifting the lid. The dumplings cook by steam — lifting the lid lets the steam escape and they will not cook properly.

content.text: go on, do not lift the lid for 15 minutes. The steam inside the covered pot is what cooks the tops of the dumplings — every time the lid comes off, the steam escapes and the dumplings take longer and cook unevenly.

If using a whole chicken, poach it in {{chicken-stock}} of stock for 35–40 minutes. Remove and shred the meat; strain and reserve the broth. Otherwise, melt {{butter-unsalted}} of butter in the casserole and cook the onion, celery, and carrot for 6–7 minutes until soft. Add the garlic for 1 minute, scatter over {{plain-flour}} of flour, stir for 1–2 minutes, then pour in the stock gradually. Simmer for 10 minutes. Add the shredded chicken, cream, and peas. Season well.

Whisk {{plain-flour}} of flour with baking powder and salt. Rub in {{butter-unsalted}} of cold butter until rough. Stir in {{whole-milk}} of milk and parsley until just combined. Drop heaped tablespoons onto the simmering stew. Cover tightly and cook for exactly 15 minutes without lifting the lid. Serve from the pot.
